title: Dhammapada
tagline: The Buddha's essential teachings on the path to liberation, in 423 memorable verses.

verses.text: Mind is the forerunner of all actions. All deeds are led by mind, created by mind. If one speaks or acts with a corrupt mind, suffering follows, as the wheel follows the hoof of the ox that draws the cart.
Mind is the forerunner of all actions. All deeds are led by mind, created by mind. If one speaks or acts with a serene mind, happiness follows, as surely as one's shadow.
"He abused me, he struck me, he defeated me, he robbed me" — in those who harbor such thoughts, hatred will never cease.
"He abused me, he struck me, he defeated me, he robbed me" — in those who do not harbor such thoughts, hatred will surely cease.
Hatred is never appeased by hatred in this world. By non-hatred alone is hatred appeased. This is an eternal law.
There are those who do not realize that one day we all must die. But those who do realize this settle their quarrels peacefully.
Whoever lives contemplating pleasant things, with senses unrestrained, in food immoderate, indolent, inactive — that one Mara surely overthrows, as the wind throws down a feeble tree.

Whoever lives contemplating the impurities of the body, with senses well-restrained, in food moderate, full of faith, full of sustained energy — that one Mara will never overthrow, any more than the wind throws down a rocky mountain.
Whoever, being depraved, devoid of self-control and truthfulness, should don the monk's yellow robe, is surely not deserving of the robe.
But whoever is purged of depravity, well-established in virtues, and filled with self-control and truthfulness, that one is indeed deserving of the yellow robe.
Those who mistake the unessential to be essential, and the essential to be unessential, dwelling in wrong thoughts, never arrive at the essential.
Those who know the essential to be essential and the unessential to be unessential, dwelling in right thoughts, do arrive at the essential.
Just as rain breaks through a poorly thatched house, so passion penetrates an undeveloped mind.
Just as rain does not break through a well-thatched house, so passion never penetrates a well-developed mind.
The evildoer grieves here and hereafter; he grieves in both worlds. He laments and is afflicted, seeing the evil of his own deeds.
The doer of good delights here and hereafter; he delights in both worlds. He delights and rejoices, seeing the purity of his own deeds.
The evildoer suffers here and hereafter; he suffers in both worlds. He suffers thinking 'I have done evil.' Still more does he suffer when gone to a state of woe.
The doer of good rejoices here and hereafter; he rejoices in both worlds. He rejoices thinking 'I have done good.' Still more does he rejoice when gone to a blissful state.
Though one may recite much of the sacred texts, if one does not act accordingly — like a cowherd who counts the cows of others — one has no share in the fruits of the holy life.

Though one may recite little of the sacred texts, if one lives the Dhamma, forsaking passion, hatred, and delusion, with right knowledge and a mind well freed, clinging to nothing here or hereafter — one shares in the fruits of the holy life.
Heedfulness is the path to the deathless. Heedlessness is the path to death. The heedful do not die. The heedless are as if already dead.
Clearly understanding this excellence of heedfulness, the wise exult therein and enjoy the resort of the noble ones.
The wise ones, ever meditative and steadfastly persevering, alone experience nibbana, the incomparable freedom from bondage.
Ever grows the glory of one who is energetic, mindful, pure in deed, considerate, self-restrained, who lives the Dhamma, and who is heedful.
By effort and heedfulness, discipline and self-mastery, let the wise one make for himself an island which no flood can overwhelm.
The foolish and ignorant indulge in heedlessness, but the wise one keeps heedfulness as his best treasure.
Do not give way to heedlessness. Do not indulge in sensual pleasures. Only the heedful and meditative attain great happiness.
Just as one upon the summit of a mountain beholds the groundlings, even so when the wise one ascends the high tower of wisdom, this sorrowless sage beholds the sorrowing and foolish multitude.
Heedful among the heedless, wide-awake among the sleepy, the wise one advances like a swift horse, leaving behind the weak jade.
By heedfulness did Indra become the overlord of the gods. Heedfulness is ever praised, and heedlessness is ever despised.
The monk who delights in heedfulness, who looks with fear on heedlessness, advances like fire, burning all fetters, small and large.
The monk who delights in heedfulness, who looks with fear on heedlessness, is not liable to fall away. He is in the presence of nibbana.
The flickering, fickle mind, difficult to guard, difficult to control — the wise one straightens it, as a fletcher straightens an arrow.
Like a fish that is drawn from its watery home and thrown upon the dry ground, this mind quivers all over in its effort to escape the dominion of Mara, the tempter.
Wonderful it is to tame the mind, so difficult to tame, ever swift, and seizing whatever it desires. A tamed mind brings happiness.
Let the wise one guard the mind, so difficult to detect and extremely subtle, seizing whatever it desires. A guarded mind brings happiness.
Dwelling in the cave of the heart, the mind, without form, wanders far and alone. Those who subdue this mind are liberated from the bonds of Mara.
For one whose mind is not steadfast, who knows not the true doctrine, whose confidence wavers — the wisdom of such a one will never be perfect.
For one whose mind is not soaked by lust, who is not afflicted in thought, who has abandoned both merit and demerit — for such a wakeful one there is no fear.

Realizing that this body is as fragile as a clay jar, and fortifying the mind like a well-fortified city, one should battle Mara with the weapon of wisdom. One should guard what has been won, without clinging.
Before long, alas, this body will lie upon the earth, unheeded, without consciousness, like a useless log cast aside.
Whatever harm an enemy may do to an enemy, or a hater to a hater, a wrongly directed mind will do one far greater harm.
Neither mother, father, nor any other relative can do one as much good as a rightly directed mind.
Who shall overcome this earth, and this realm of Yama, and this world of gods? Who shall select a well-taught verse of truth, even as an expert garland-maker selects flowers?

A disciple in training shall overcome this earth, and this realm of Yama, and this world of gods. A disciple in training shall select a well-taught verse of truth, even as an expert garland-maker selects flowers.
Knowing that this body is like foam, realizing that it is as insubstantial as a mirage, one should destroy the flower-tipped arrows of Mara and pass beyond the sight of the King of Death.
As a flood sweeps away a sleeping village, so death carries away the person of distracted mind who only gathers the flowers of sense pleasures.
The Destroyer brings under his sway the person of distracted mind who, insatiate in sense desires, only gathers the flowers of pleasure.
As a bee, having gathered nectar from a flower, flies away without harming the flower, its color, or its fragrance, so should a sage wander through a village.
Do not give attention to what others have done or not done; give attention to what you yourself have done and have not done.
Like a beautiful flower, full of color but lacking fragrance, are the fair but fruitless words of one who does not act accordingly.
Like a beautiful flower, full of color and also fragrant, are the fair and fruitful words of one who acts accordingly.
As from a heap of flowers many a garland can be made, so by one born mortal should many good deeds be done.

The fragrance of flowers does not travel against the wind, nor does the fragrance of sandalwood or jasmine. But the fragrance of the virtuous travels against the wind. The virtuous one pervades all directions with the fragrance of virtue.
Of all the fragrances — of sandalwood, tagara, blue lotus, and jasmine — the fragrance of virtue is by far the sweetest.
Faint is the fragrance of tagara and sandalwood; but the fragrance of the virtuous is supreme, spreading even to the gods.
Mara never finds the path of those who are virtuous, heedful, and freed by perfect knowledge.

Just as on a rubbish heap by the side of the highway a lotus may grow, sweet-smelling and pleasing — even so, among the rubbish heap of blind mortals, a disciple of the Fully Enlightened One shines with wisdom.
Long is the night to one who is wakeful; long is a league to one who is weary of the journey; long is the cycle of rebirth to the foolish ones who do not know the true Dhamma.
If on one's way one does not come upon one's better or one's equal, let one firmly go on one's way alone; there is no companionship with the foolish.
"These sons belong to me, and this wealth belongs to me" — with such thoughts a fool is tormented. He himself does not belong to himself; how much less sons and wealth?
The fool who knows his foolishness is wise at least so far. But a fool who thinks himself wise — he is called a fool indeed.
If a fool be associated with a wise person even all his life, he will perceive the truth as little as a spoon perceives the taste of soup.
If an intelligent person be associated for even one moment with a wise person, he will quickly perceive the truth, as the tongue perceives the taste of soup.
Fools of deficient wisdom are their own greatest enemies, for they do evil deeds that bear bitter fruit.
That deed is not well done which, having been done, brings remorse, whose result one receives weeping and with a tearful face.
That deed is well done which, having been done, brings no remorse, whose result one receives gladly and happily.
As long as an evil deed has not ripened, the fool thinks it is sweet as honey. But when the evil deed ripens, the fool comes to grief.
To his own ruin the fool gains knowledge, for it cleaves his head and destroys his innate goodness.

One is the road that leads to worldly gain, and another is the road that leads to nibbana. Clearly understanding this, the monk who is a disciple of the Buddha should not rejoice in worldly favors, but should cultivate detachment.

Should one find a person who points out faults and who reproves, let one follow such a wise and sagacious counselor as one would a guide to hidden treasure. It is always better, and never worse, to cultivate such an association.
Let him admonish, let him teach, let him forbid what is improper! — he will be loved by the good and hated by the bad.
Do not associate with evil companions; do not seek the fellowship of the vile. Associate with the good friends; seek the fellowship of noble persons.
One who drinks in the Dhamma lives happily with a serene mind. The wise one ever delights in the Dhamma made known by the noble ones.
Irrigators regulate the waters, fletchers straighten arrow shafts, carpenters shape wood, and the wise control themselves.
Just as a solid rock is not shaken by the wind, even so the wise are not ruffled by praise or blame.
On hearing the Dhamma, the wise become as clear and calm as a deep, smooth, still lake.
Good people let go of everything. The virtuous do not prattle with a yearning for pleasures. When touched by happiness or sorrow, the wise show no elation or depression.

Neither for the sake of oneself nor for the sake of another should a person do what is wrong, should not desire son, wealth, or kingdom by doing wrong. Such a one is truly virtuous, wise, and righteous.
Few among people are those who cross to the farther shore. The rest, the bulk of people, only run up and down along this shore.
But those who act according to the perfectly taught Dhamma will cross the realm of death, so difficult to cross.

Those whose minds have reached full excellence in the factors of enlightenment, who, having renounced acquisitiveness, rejoice in not clinging to things — such are the ones with taints destroyed, full of light, who have attained nibbana even in this world.
Better than a thousand hollow words is one word that brings peace.
Better than a thousand hollow verses is one verse that brings peace.
Better than reciting a hundred meaningless verses is the reciting of one verse of Dhamma, hearing which one attains peace.
Though one may conquer a thousand times a thousand men in battle, yet he indeed is the noblest victor who conquers himself.
Self-conquest is far better than the conquest of others. Not even a god, an angel, Mara, or Brahma can turn into defeat the victory of a person who is self-subdued and ever restrained in conduct.
Whatever gifts and oblations one may offer in this world for an entire year in order to gain merit — the whole of it is not worth a quarter of the merit gained by revering those who are upright.
For one who is in the habit of constantly honoring and respecting the elders, four blessings increase: long life, beauty, happiness, and strength.
Better it is to live one day virtuous and meditative than to live a hundred years immoral and uncontrolled.
Better it is to live one day wise and meditative than to live a hundred years foolish and uncontrolled.
Better it is to live one day strenuous and resolute than to live a hundred years sluggish and dissipated.
Better it is to live one day seeing the rise and fall of things than to live a hundred years without ever seeing the rise and fall of things.
Better it is to live one day seeing the deathless than to live a hundred years without ever seeing the deathless.
Better it is to live one day seeing the supreme truth than to live a hundred years without ever seeing the supreme truth.
When this world is ever ablaze, why this laughter, why this jubilation? Shrouded in darkness, will you not seek the light?
Behold this body — a painted image, a mass of heaped-up sores, infirm, full of hankering — of which nothing is lasting or stable!
Fully worn out is this body, a nest of disease and fragile. This foul mass breaks up, for death is the end of life.
This city of bones is plastered over with flesh and blood; within it dwell old age and death, pride and jealousy.
Even gorgeous royal chariots wear out, and the body too wears out. But the Dhamma of the good does not age; thus the good make it known among the good.
A person of little learning grows old like a bull. His flesh may increase, but his knowledge does not grow.
Through many a birth in samsara have I wandered in vain, seeking the builder of this house of life. Repeated birth is indeed suffering!

O house-builder, you are seen! You shall build no house again. All your rafters are broken, your ridgepole is sundered. My mind has reached the unconditioned; I have attained the destruction of craving.
Not to do any evil, to cultivate good, to purify one's mind — this is the teaching of the Buddhas.
Patient endurance is the supreme austerity. Nibbana is supreme, say the Buddhas. One who harms another is not a true renunciate; one who oppresses others is not a true ascetic.
Not insulting, not harming, restraint according to the rules of the order, moderation in food, dwelling in solitude, and devotion to meditation — this is the teaching of the Buddhas.

Not by a rain of golden coins can sensual desires ever be satisfied. Sensual pleasures give little sweetness and are fraught with pain. Knowing this, the wise person finds no delight even in heavenly pleasures. The disciple of the Supreme Buddha delights in the destruction of craving.

One who has gone for refuge to the Buddha, the Dhamma, and the Sangha, penetrates with transcendental wisdom the Four Noble Truths — suffering, the cause of suffering, the cessation of suffering, and the Noble Eightfold Path leading to the cessation of suffering.
This indeed is the safe refuge; this is the refuge supreme. Having gone to such a refuge, one is released from all suffering.
Hard to find is a person of great wisdom: such a one is not born everywhere. Where such a wise one is born, that family prospers happily.
Happy is the arising of the Buddhas. Happy is the teaching of the true Dhamma. Happy is the unity of the Sangha. Happy is the discipline of the united ones.
Happy indeed we live, friendly amidst the hostile. Amidst hostile people we dwell free from hatred.
Happy indeed we live, healthy amidst the afflicted. Amidst afflicted people we dwell free from affliction.
Happy indeed we live, content amidst the greedy. Amidst greedy people we dwell free from greed.
Happy indeed we live, we who possess nothing. Feeders on joy we shall be, like the Radiant Gods.
Victory breeds hatred. The defeated live in pain. Happily the peaceful live, giving up both victory and defeat.
There is no fire like lust, no crime like hatred, no sorrow like the body in its constituents, no happiness higher than peace.
Hunger is the worst disease. Compounded things are the greatest suffering. Knowing this as it really is, the wise realize nibbana, the highest happiness.
Health is the greatest gift, contentment is the greatest wealth, a trusted friend is the best relative, nibbana is the greatest happiness.
Having tasted the flavor of solitude and the flavor of peace, one becomes free from distress and free from evil, drinking the flavor of the joy of the Dhamma.
Of all the paths, the Eightfold Path is the best; of all the truths, the Four Noble Truths are the best; of all things, detachment is the best; of all persons, the seeing one is the best.
This is the only path; there is none other for the purification of vision. Enter upon this path, which is the bewilderment of Mara.
Entering upon this path, you will make an end of suffering. Having myself known this path, which removes all thorns, I have taught it to you.
You yourselves must strive; the Buddhas only point the way. Those meditative ones who tread the path are released from the bonds of Mara.
"All conditioned things are impermanent" — when one sees this with wisdom, one turns away from suffering. This is the path to purification.
"All conditioned things are unsatisfactory" — when one sees this with wisdom, one turns away from suffering. This is the path to purification.
"All things are not-self" — when one sees this with wisdom, one turns away from suffering. This is the path to purification.
The idler who does not strive when he should strive, who though young and strong is given to sloth, whose will and thought are weak — that lazy, idle person will never find the path to wisdom.
Watchful of speech, well restrained in mind, let one do nothing unskillful through the body. Let one purify these three courses of action and win the path made known by the sages.
Wisdom springs from meditation; without meditation wisdom wanes. Having known this twofold path of gain and loss, let one so conduct oneself that wisdom may increase.
Strive and cut off the stream of craving! Discard, O brahmin, sense desires! When you have understood the destruction of all that is conditioned, you are one who knows the uncreated.
One for whom there is neither this shore nor the other, nor both, who is undistressed and unbound — that one I call a holy person.
The sun shines by day, the moon shines by night, the warrior shines in armor, the brahmin shines in meditation. But all day and all night the Buddha shines in radiance.
One who does no evil through body, speech, or mind, who is restrained in these three ways — that one I call a holy person.
Not by matted hair, not by lineage, not by birth is one a holy person. One in whom there is truth and righteousness — that one is blessed, that one is a holy person.

I do not call one a holy person merely by being born of a certain family or mother. If one is full of impediments, one is just someone who says 'sir.' Only one who is free from impediments, free from clinging — that one I call a holy person.
One who bears abuse without resentment — who is patient, strong, and pure in the power of holiness — that one I call a holy person.

One who has passed beyond this miry path, this difficult road and ocean of delusion, who has crossed to the other shore, who meditates, is free from craving and doubt, who clings to nothing and has attained nibbana — that one I call a holy person.
One whose destiny no one knows — neither gods, nor angels, nor humans — one whose taints are destroyed, who has attained sainthood — that one I call a holy person.

One who knows one's former births, who sees the heavens and the states of woe, who has reached the end of births, who has perfected oneself in wisdom, who has completed the holy life — that one I call a holy person.

title: Heart Sutra
tagline: The essence of the Perfection of Wisdom — form is emptiness, emptiness is form.

sections.heading: The Heart of the Perfection of Wisdom

verses.text: Thus have I heard. At one time the Blessed One was dwelling in Rajagriha at Vulture Peak Mountain, together with a great gathering of the sangha of monks and a great gathering of the sangha of bodhisattvas.

At that time the Blessed One entered the samadhi that expresses the dharma called Profound Illumination, and at the same time noble Avalokiteshvara, the bodhisattva mahasattva, while practicing the profound perfection of wisdom, saw in this way: he saw the five aggregates to be empty of nature.

Then, through the power of the Buddha, venerable Shariputra said to noble Avalokiteshvara, the bodhisattva mahasattva: "How should a son or daughter of noble family train, who wishes to practice the profound perfection of wisdom?"

Avalokiteshvara, the bodhisattva mahasattva, said to venerable Shariputra: "O Shariputra, a son or daughter of noble family who wishes to practice the profound perfection of wisdom should see in this way: seeing the five aggregates to be empty of nature."
"Form is emptiness; emptiness also is form. Emptiness is no other than form; form is no other than emptiness. In the same way, feeling, perception, formation, and consciousness are emptiness."
"Thus, Shariputra, all dharmas are emptiness. There are no characteristics. There is no birth and no cessation. There is no impurity and no purity. There is no decrease and no increase."

"Therefore, Shariputra, in emptiness there is no form, no feeling, no perception, no formation, no consciousness; no eye, no ear, no nose, no tongue, no body, no mind; no appearance, no sound, no smell, no taste, no touch, no dharmas."
"There is no eye element up to no mind consciousness element. There is no ignorance, no end of ignorance up to no old age and death, no end of old age and death."
"There is no suffering, no origin of suffering, no cessation of suffering, no path, no wisdom, no attainment, and no non-attainment."

"Therefore, Shariputra, since the bodhisattvas have no attainment, they abide by means of the perfection of wisdom. Since there is no obscuration of mind, there is no fear. They transcend falsity and attain complete nirvana."
"All the buddhas of the three times, by means of the perfection of wisdom, fully awaken to unsurpassable, true, complete enlightenment."

"Therefore, the great mantra of the perfection of wisdom, the mantra of great insight, the unsurpassed mantra, the unequaled mantra, the mantra that calms all suffering, should be known as truth, since there is no deception."
"The perfection of wisdom mantra is said in this way: Gate gate paragate parasamgate bodhi svaha. Gone, gone, gone beyond, gone completely beyond — awakening! So be it."

Then the Blessed One arose from that samadhi and praised noble Avalokiteshvara, the bodhisattva mahasattva, saying: "Good, good, O son of noble family; thus it is, O son of noble family, thus it is. One should practice the profound perfection of wisdom just as you have taught, and all the tathagatas will rejoice."

When the Blessed One had said this, venerable Shariputra and noble Avalokiteshvara, the bodhisattva mahasattva, and the whole assembly and the world with its gods, humans, asuras, and gandharvas rejoiced and praised the words of the Blessed One.

title: Diamond Sutra
tagline: A dialogue on the nature of perception, reality, and the perfection of wisdom.

verses.text: Thus have I heard. At one time the Buddha was staying in the Jeta Grove near the city of Shravasti, together with a community of twelve hundred and fifty monks.
One day, at mealtime, the World-Honored One put on his robe and, carrying his bowl, went into the great city of Shravasti to beg for food, going from house to house.
When he had finished eating, he put away his robe and bowl, washed his feet, arranged his seat, and sat down.

Then the elder Subhuti, who was among the assembly, rose from his seat, bared his right shoulder, knelt upon his right knee, and with palms joined respectfully, addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, it is most rare and wonderful how well the Tathagata protects and is mindful of all bodhisattvas, and how well he instructs them."
"World-Honored One, if good men and good women seek the supreme awakening, upon what should they rely, and how should they subdue their thoughts?"

The Buddha said: "Very good, Subhuti! It is just as you say. The Tathagata is well-mindful of all bodhisattvas and well instructs them. Now listen carefully and I shall tell you. Good men and good women who seek the supreme awakening should thus subdue their thoughts."
"Yes, World-Honored One, we eagerly wish to hear," replied Subhuti.

The Buddha said to Subhuti: "All the bodhisattva-mahasattvas should subdue their thoughts in the following manner: Of all sentient beings — whether born from eggs, from wombs, from moisture, or by transformation; whether with form or without form; whether with thought or without thought, or with neither thought nor non-thought — I cause them all to enter nirvana without remainder, and so liberate them."

"Yet when vast, uncountable, immeasurable numbers of beings have thus been liberated, truly no being has been liberated. Why is this so, Subhuti? If a bodhisattva holds to the notion of a self, a person, a sentient being, or a life span, that one is not a true bodhisattva."

"Furthermore, Subhuti, a bodhisattva who practices generosity should not dwell in any object when practicing generosity. That is to say, one should give without dwelling in forms, without dwelling in sounds, smells, tastes, tactile objects, or mental objects. Subhuti, a bodhisattva should give without dwelling in any notion of a sign."

"Why is this? If a bodhisattva practices generosity without dwelling in any sign, the merit of that act is not something that can be conceived or measured. Subhuti, what do you think? Can one measure all of space in the eastern direction?" "No, World-Honored One." "Subhuti, can one measure space in the southern, western, or northern directions, or in any of the four intermediate directions, or above, or below?" "No, World-Honored One." "Subhuti, the merit of a bodhisattva who gives without dwelling in signs is just as inconceivable as that."

"Subhuti, what do you think? Can the Tathagata be recognized by means of bodily form?" "No, World-Honored One. The Tathagata cannot be recognized by means of bodily form. Why? Because what the Tathagata calls bodily form is not truly bodily form."
The Buddha said to Subhuti: "All that has form is illusive and unreal. When you see that all forms are illusory and unreal, then you will begin to perceive your true Buddha nature."
"Wherever there is form, there is delusion. If you can see that all forms are non-forms, then you see the Tathagata."

sections.heading: Section 6: The Rarity of True Faith

verses.text: Subhuti asked the Buddha: "World-Honored One, will there always be beings who, upon hearing these words, will truly have faith?" The Buddha said to Subhuti: "Do not speak in that way. Even after the Tathagata has been gone for five hundred years, there will be those who keep the moral precepts and cultivate blessings who will have faith in these words and accept them as truth."

"You should know that such persons have not merely planted seeds of virtue under one buddha, two buddhas, three, four, or five buddhas, but have, in truth, planted seeds of virtue under countless tens of thousands of buddhas. Anyone who, upon hearing these words, gives rise to a single moment of genuine faith, Subhuti — the Tathagata sees them and knows them, and they gain immeasurable merit."

"Why is this? Because these beings no longer grasp at a self, a person, a sentient being, or a life span. They no longer grasp at dharmas, and they no longer grasp at non-dharmas. Why? Because if beings grasp at the notion of a dharma, they are still clinging to a self, a person, a sentient being, and a life span."
"That is why the Tathagata always says: 'You monks should know that the dharma I teach is like a raft.' Even the dharma should be abandoned, how much more so non-dharma."

sections.heading: Section 10: Setting Forth the Pure Land

verses.text: The Buddha asked Subhuti: "In ancient times, when the Tathagata was with Dipankara Buddha, did he attain anything in the teaching?" "No, World-Honored One. When the Tathagata was with Dipankara Buddha, in truth there was nothing attained in the teaching."
"Subhuti, what do you think? Does a bodhisattva adorn the Buddha lands?" "No, World-Honored One. Why? Because adorning the Buddha lands is not truly adorning; it is merely called adorning."

"Therefore, Subhuti, all bodhisattva-mahasattvas should give rise to a pure and clear mind that does not dwell in form, sound, smell, taste, touch, or mental objects. They should give rise to a mind that does not dwell anywhere."

"Subhuti, if a person had a body as great as Mount Sumeru, what do you think — would that body be great?" Subhuti said: "Very great, World-Honored One. Why? Because the Buddha teaches that what is called a great body is not truly a great body. That is what is called a great body."

sections.heading: Section 14: Perfect Peace in the Cutting Off of All Notions

verses.text: At that time, having heard this teaching and having deeply understood its meaning, Subhuti wept and said: "How rare, World-Honored One! The Buddha has taught this exceedingly profound sutra. Since the day I attained the wisdom eye, I have never heard a teaching so profound as this."
"World-Honored One, if someone hears this sutra and has faith in it with a pure and clear mind, that person gives rise to true perception. This person achieves the rarest of all merits."
"World-Honored One, this true perception is not, in fact, a perception. That is what the Tathagata calls a true perception."

"World-Honored One, it is not difficult for me to have faith in, understand, and put into practice this sutra that I have heard today. But in the future, in five hundred years, if there are beings who hear this sutra, have faith in it, understand it, and practice it, such people will be most rare."

"Why? Because they will be free from the notion of a self, the notion of a person, the notion of a sentient being, and the notion of a life span. Why? The notion of a self is not a notion. The notion of a person, a sentient being, or a life span is not a notion. Why? Because having left all notions behind, one is called a buddha."

At that time Subhuti asked the Buddha: "World-Honored One, if good men and good women seek the supreme awakening, how should they abide and how should they master their thoughts?" The Buddha told Subhuti: "Good men and good women who seek the supreme awakening should give rise to this thought: I should liberate all sentient beings, yet when all sentient beings have been liberated, in truth no sentient being has been liberated."

"Why is this so? Subhuti, if a bodhisattva holds to the notion of a self, a person, a sentient being, or a life span, then that one is not a bodhisattva. Why? Subhuti, in reality there is no such thing as the supreme awakening."

"Subhuti, what do you think? When the Tathagata was with Dipankara Buddha, was there any attainment of the supreme awakening?" "No, World-Honored One. As I understand the meaning of the Buddha's teaching, when the Buddha was with Dipankara Buddha there was no attainment of the supreme awakening."

The Buddha said: "Just so, just so. Subhuti, in truth there is no such thing as the supreme awakening. Subhuti, if there were, Dipankara Buddha would not have given me the prediction: 'In your next life you shall become a buddha called Shakyamuni.' It is precisely because there is no fixed dharma called the supreme awakening that Dipankara Buddha gave me this prediction."

"Why is this? The one called Tathagata is the suchness of all dharmas. If someone were to say the Tathagata has attained the supreme awakening, Subhuti, there is in truth no dharma by which the Buddha has attained the supreme awakening. The supreme awakening attained by the Tathagata is neither real nor unreal."

"Subhuti, if someone were to fill the three thousand great thousand worlds with the seven precious treasures and give them all as a gift, the merit gained by this person would not equal that of one who receives, upholds, studies, and recites even a four-line verse of this teaching and explains it to others."
"How should it be explained to others? By not grasping at any appearance, by remaining unmoved by appearances, by abiding in the suchness of things. Why is this?"
"All conditioned phenomena are like a dream, like an illusion, like a bubble, like a shadow, like a dewdrop, like a flash of lightning. One should contemplate them in this way."

When the Buddha finished this discourse, the elder Subhuti, together with the monks, nuns, laymen, laywomen, and all the beings of the world — the gods, humans, and asuras — having heard what the Buddha had said, were filled with great joy, accepted it with faith, and put it into practice.

title: Platform Sutra of the Sixth Patriarch
tagline: The Sixth Patriarch's revolutionary teaching on sudden awakening and original nature.

verses.text: Good friends, purify your minds and concentrate on the teaching of the great perfection of wisdom. The Great Master stopped speaking and quieted his own mind. After a good while he said: Good friends, please listen. My father was originally from Fanyang. He was banished to Lingnan and became a commoner in Xinzhou. My father died when I was young, and my aged mother and I, her only son, moved to Nanhai. We suffered extreme poverty, and I sold firewood in the marketplace.

By chance a man bought some firewood and had me deliver it to his shop. After he received it and I was paid, as I went out the door I saw a man reciting a sutra. When I heard the words of this sutra, my mind immediately became clear and I was awakened. I asked him: "What sutra are you reciting?" He answered: "The Diamond Sutra." I then asked: "Where do you come from, and why do you recite this sutra?" He said: "I come from the East Meditation Monastery in Huangmei district. The Fifth Patriarch, Hongren, is the master there, with over a thousand disciples."

I then traveled to Huangmei and called on the Fifth Patriarch. The Patriarch asked me: "Where are you from, and what do you seek?" I answered: "Your disciple is a commoner from Lingnan, from Xinzhou. I have come a great distance to pay homage to you, and I seek nothing other than buddhahood." The Patriarch said: "You are from Lingnan and, furthermore, you are a barbarian. How can you become a buddha?" I replied: "Although people exist as northerners and southerners, the buddha-nature fundamentally has no north or south. The body of a barbarian and that of a monk are not the same, but what difference is there in our buddha-nature?"

The Fifth Patriarch wished to have the assembly write verses so he could see their understanding. He said: "If anyone comprehends the great meaning, he shall receive the robe and the Dharma and become the Sixth Patriarch." The head monk Shenxiu wrote on the corridor wall: "The body is the bodhi tree, the mind is like a bright mirror's stand. At all times we must strive to polish it, and must not let dust collect."

I, Huineng, also composed a verse and asked someone who could write to post it on the west corridor wall, so that I might offer what my original mind had to say. If you do not know the original mind, learning the Dharma is to no avail. If you know the mind and see its true nature, you then awaken to the great meaning. My verse said: "Bodhi originally has no tree. The bright mirror also has no stand. Fundamentally there is not a single thing — where could dust arise?"

The Fifth Patriarch knew that I had a splendid understanding of the highest meaning. Fearing that others might harm me, he came to me at midnight in the threshing room, where I was pounding rice. He taught me the Diamond Sutra, and when he came to the passage "Give rise to a mind that does not dwell anywhere," I was at once greatly enlightened, and realized that all the myriad dharmas are not separate from one's own nature. The Patriarch then transmitted the Dharma and the robe to me, saying: "You are now the Sixth Patriarch. Guard this teaching well and do not let it come to an end. Depart now — go south and hide, for danger is near."

Good friends, the wisdom of enlightenment and prajna are inherently possessed by all people of the world. It is only because the mind is deluded that people cannot attain realization by themselves. They must seek the help of a good and wise teacher to see into their own nature. Good friends, in the moment of a single thought of enlightenment, all sentient beings become buddhas.

Good friends, prajna is the wisdom of enlightenment. If at all times your every thought contains no ignorance, and you always act with wisdom, then this is the practice of prajna. With a single foolish thought, prajna is cut off. With a single wise thought, prajna is born. People in the world are deluded and cannot see prajna. They speak of it with their mouths but their minds remain ignorant. It is like someone who always talks about food but never eats — even after ten thousand years, that person would never be satisfied.

Good friends, maha-prajna-paramita is a Sanskrit word that means "great wisdom to reach the other shore." This must be practiced in the mind and not merely recited in words. Reciting it without practicing it is like a phantom, an illusion, a dewdrop, a flash of lightning. When recitation and practice correspond to each other, your own dharma body is the buddha.

What is maha? Maha means great. The capacity of the mind is vast and great like empty space. It has no boundaries, no square or round, no great or small, no blue or yellow, no red or white, no above or below, no long or short, no anger or joy, no right or wrong, no good or evil, no first or last. The buddha lands are all the same as empty space.

Good friends, do not listen to my explanation of emptiness and then attach to emptiness. If you sit still with an empty mind, you are attached to undifferentiated emptiness. Good friends, the emptiness of the universe can contain the forms and shapes of the ten thousand things: the sun, moon, and stars; the mountains, rivers, and streams; the grasses, trees, and forests; good people and bad people; good dharmas and bad dharmas; heavens and hells. All of these are within emptiness. The emptiness of people's nature is just like this.

Good friends, in my teaching, meditation and wisdom are the foundation. But do not be under the mistaken impression that meditation and wisdom are different. Meditation and wisdom are of one essence, not two. Meditation is the essence of wisdom, and wisdom is the function of meditation. At the very moment of wisdom, meditation exists in that wisdom. At the very moment of meditation, wisdom exists in that meditation.

Good friends, this means that meditation and wisdom are alike. Students of the Way, take heed: do not say that meditation gives rise to wisdom, or that wisdom gives rise to meditation, or that meditation and wisdom are different from each other. To hold this view implies that dharmas possess duality.

If the mouth speaks good words but the mind has bad thoughts, then meditation and wisdom are useless because they are not equal. If mind and mouth are both good, and the inner and the outer are one, then meditation and wisdom are equal.

The practice of self-awakening does not consist of arguing. If you argue about which comes first, meditation or wisdom, you are like a deluded person. Without settling the argument you will give rise to notions of winner and loser and will not abandon the idea of a self and of dharmas.

Good friends, what are meditation and wisdom like? They are like a lamp and its light. If there is a lamp, there is light. If there is no lamp, there is darkness. The lamp is the essence of the light, and the light is the function of the lamp. Although they have two names, their fundamental nature is one. Meditation and wisdom are just like this.

Good friends, in this teaching of mine, from ancient times up to the present, all have set up no-thought as the main doctrine, non-form as the substance, and non-abiding as the basis. Non-form is to be separated from form even when associated with form. No-thought is not to think even when involved in thought. Non-abiding is the original nature of humankind.

In regard to all things, thought after thought does not attach. If your one thought attaches, then successive thoughts attach — this is called being fettered. If in regard to all dharmas your successive thoughts do not attach, then you are unfettered. Therefore, non-abiding is made the basis.
Good friends, being outwardly separated from all forms, this is non-form. When you are separated from form, the substance of your nature is pure. Therefore, non-form is made the substance.

Good friends, no-thought means: even when involved in thought, you do not think of the myriad things. Not that you drive away thoughts — if you stop all thoughts, you die and are reborn elsewhere. Students of the Way, take heed! Do not fail to understand the meaning of the dharma. If you yourself are deluded, that is bad enough; how much worse to encourage others in their delusions! Not only does the deluded person not realize he is deluded, but he slanders the sutras and the dharma.

Good friends, why is no-thought established as the main doctrine? It is because deluded people think in terms of objects, and from these thoughts wrong views arise. All afflictions and false thinking arise from this. However, this school establishes no-thought as its doctrine. People of the world, separate yourselves from views! Do not give rise to thoughts based on views. If there were no thinking at all, no-thought would have no place to exist.

The self-nature of suchness gives rise to thought. Even though the eye, ear, nose, and tongue perceive forms, sounds, smells, and tastes, the self-nature is not stained. This original nature is forever free and pure, and the myriad dharmas are within your self-nature. If you think of the myriad things with an evil mind, you will practice evil. If you think of the myriad things with a good mind, you will practice good. Thus it is clear that all dharmas exist within your own mind. Your own mind is itself the buddha.

title: Metta Sutta
tagline: The Buddha's discourse on universal loving-kindness — a practice of boundless goodwill.

verses.text: This is what should be done by one who is skilled in goodness, and who knows the path of peace: let them be able and upright, straightforward and gentle in speech, humble and not conceited.
Contented and easily satisfied, unburdened with duties and frugal in their ways, peaceful and calm, and wise and skillful, not proud or demanding in nature.
Let them not do the slightest thing that the wise would later reprove. Wishing: in gladness and in safety, may all beings be at ease.
Whatever living beings there may be — whether they are weak or strong, omitting none, the great or the mighty, medium, short, or small, the seen and the unseen.
Those living near and far away, those born and to be born — may all beings be at ease.
Let none deceive another, or despise any being in any state. Let none through anger or ill-will wish harm upon another.
Even as a mother protects with her life her child, her only child, so with a boundless heart should one cherish all living beings.
Radiating kindness over the entire world: spreading upwards to the skies, and downwards to the depths, outwards and unbounded, freed from hatred and ill-will.
Whether standing or walking, seated or lying down, free from drowsiness, one should sustain this recollection. This is said to be the sublime abiding.
By not holding to fixed views, the pure-hearted one, having clarity of vision, being freed from all sense-desires, is not born again into this world.

title: Song of Mahamudra
tagline: Tilopa's oral instructions to Naropa on the nature of mind — direct and uncompromising.

verses.text: Mahamudra cannot be taught, but most intelligent Naropa, since you have undergone rigorous austerity, with forbearance in suffering and devotion to your guru, blessed one, take this to heart.

Look at the nature of the world: nothing lasts, nothing is real, all is like a dream and an illusion. Dream and illusion — even these are meaningless. Therefore, give up the ways of the world and renounce all desire.

Cut off all attachment to friends, to kin, to country. Meditate alone in the forests, in retreats, in mountain caves. Remain in the state of non-meditation. If you attain non-attainment, then you have attained mahamudra.
The void needs no reliance; mahamudra rests on nothing. Without making an effort, but remaining loose and natural, one can break the yoke, thus gaining liberation.
If you see nothing when you stare into empty space, if with the mind you then observe the mind, you destroy distinctions and reach buddhahood.
The clouds that wander through the sky have no roots, no home; nor do the distinctive thoughts floating through the mind. Once the self-mind is seen, discrimination stops.
In space, shapes and colors form, but neither by black nor by white is space tinged. From the self-mind all things emerge; the mind by virtues and by vices is not stained.
Do not imagine, do not think, do not analyze, do not meditate, do not reflect. Keep the mind in its natural state.

The darkness of ages cannot shroud the glowing sun; the long ages of samsara cannot hide the splendid light of the mind. Though words are spoken to explain the void, the void as such can never be expressed.
Though we say "the mind is a bright light," it is beyond all words and symbols. Although the mind is void in essence, all things it embraces and contains.

Do nothing with the body but relax. Shut firm the mouth and keep silent. Empty your mind and think of nothing. Like a hollow bamboo, rest at ease with your body. Giving not, nor taking, put your mind at rest. Mahamudra is like a mind that clings to nothing. Thus practicing, in time you will reach buddhahood.

The practice of mantra and paramita, instruction in the sutras and precepts, and teaching from the schools and scriptures will not bring realization of the innate truth. For if the mind when filled with some desire should seek a goal, it only hides the light.

One who keeps tantric precepts yet discriminates betrays the spirit of samaya. Cease all activity, abandon all desire, let thoughts rise and fall as they will, like the ocean waves. One who never harms the non-abiding, nor the principle of non-distinction, upholds the tantric precepts.

One who abandons craving and clings not to this or that, perceives the real meaning given in the scriptures. In mahamudra all one's sins are burned; in mahamudra one is released from the prison of this world. This is the dharma's supreme torch.

Those who are ignorant of this, and those who are not devoted, are swept along by the stream of ever-returning sorrow and suffering. Those who are ignorant and not devoted are to be pitied, for they undergo unbearable and ceaseless suffering.
If you wish to free yourself from ceaseless suffering, rely upon a wise and learned guru. When the guru's blessing enters your heart, your own mind is liberated.

All things in this world are fleeting; all are like dreams and like magical apparitions. Dream and apparition — even these are meaningless. Therefore, give up the life of the worldling. Cut the fetters of attachment and revulsion, and meditate alone in forests and mountain retreats.

When you abide in the state that is beyond meditation, if you find there is nothing to attain — then you have attained mahamudra. The tree of true devotion spreads its branches wide; the tree of mahamudra bears the fruit of great compassion.

The joy that reaches beyond all pleasure, the happiness of supreme liberation — this is the great bliss of mahamudra. There is no realization beyond this. Transcending the limitations of the scholar and the monastery, this teaching comes directly from the guru to the worthy disciple.

Space is beyond color and shape; it is not affected by black or white. Mind essence is beyond color and shape; it is not affected by virtues or vices. The clear light of the sun cannot be dimmed by the darkness of a thousand ages.

Naropa, this supreme essence has been transmitted. The limitless clear light of the mind cannot be hidden by the darkness of samsara, not for even an age. This song of mahamudra was composed by Tilopa on the banks of the Ganges after he had given mind-transmission to Naropa and had led him through twelve great hardships.

title: The Way of the Bodhisattva
tagline: Shantideva's masterwork on compassion, patience, and the bodhisattva's path to awakening.

verses.text: To those who go in bliss, the dharma they have taught, and to all their heirs, to all who merit veneration, I bow down with devotion, body, speech, and mind.

This free, well-favored human form is difficult to obtain. Now that you have the chance to realize the full human potential, if you don't make good use of this opportunity, how could you possibly expect to have such a chance again?

As a flash of lightning on a dark, cloudy night for an instant brightly illuminates all, so in this world, through the might of the Buddha, a wholesome thought rises for an instant in the minds of people.
Thus the power of good is always weak, while the power of evil is vast and terrible. What other virtue could overcome it, if not the perfect awakening mind?
For like the supreme substance of the alchemists, this unclean body, taken as it is, will be transformed into the priceless body of a buddha. So firmly seize this thing called bodhicitta.

If the boundless leaders of the world, who are the only unerring judges of what is of value, have well considered and found that bodhicitta is of inestimable worth — then you who wish for freedom, hold it firmly.
The moment bodhicitta is born in the wretched ones chained in the prison of samsara, they are called the children of the buddhas, and become worthy of veneration by both humans and gods.

Just as the plantain tree bears fruit and then is destroyed, so it is with the thought of the ordinary person. But the wish for enlightenment, like a great wishing tree, bears fruit unceasingly and is never exhausted, but grows through being shared.
Today, in the presence of all the protectors, I invite the world to be my guests at the feast of temporary and ultimate happiness. May gods, demigods, and all others rejoice!

Just as the previous Sugatas gave birth to bodhicitta and progressively trained in the practices of a bodhisattva, so too for the benefit of beings will I give birth to bodhicitta and progressively train in those very practices.
Having thus seized this precious mind with lucidity, the wise person should always cultivate it to bring it to fulfillment.
From today onward, to accomplish the welfare of beings, even though sleeping or apparently unconcerned, a force of merit equal to the sky will perpetually increase.

Those who wish to train in the way of the bodhisattvas, and who have embraced the training that they must observe, should guard their minds with great care, for without this vigilance, the training cannot be maintained.
Untamed elephants in their fury do not cause as much destruction as the unrestrained mind inflicts upon us in the hells and elsewhere — the very worst of torments.
But if the elephant of the mind is held firmly on all sides by the rope of mindfulness, all fear will vanish and every virtue will come to hand.
So with my palms pressed together I make my earnest prayer: always to place a guard, the sentry of awareness, upon the threshold of my mind.
Why do I not understand that all the pain and misery I suffer comes from my own mind? In the hells and elsewhere, the only cause of all my pain has always been my undisciplined mind.
If with the rope of mindfulness I bind this elephant of the mind, all dangers will be averted and all virtues gained.

Tigers, lions, elephants, bears, serpents, and all enemies, the guardians of the hells, witches, and demons too — all these are bound by binding the mind alone. By taming the mind alone, all these are tamed.
All the good works gathered in a thousand ages, such as deeds of generosity and offerings to the blissful ones — a single flash of anger shatters them.
There is no evil like hatred, and no fortitude like patience. Thus I should strive in various ways to meditate on patience.
If there is a remedy when trouble strikes, what reason is there for dejection? And if there is no remedy for it, what use is there in being glum?
Those tormented by the fires of anger find no peace of mind. Strangers to every joy and pleasure, they can neither sleep nor feel secure.
Even those whom they have helped with gifts and honors will turn against them and even take their lives — for angry people alienate their friends.

Those who harm me come against me, driven by conditions; they do not come of their own free will. It is like a sickness — it arises without being wanted. No one wants to be sick, and yet disease occurs.
If even those who injure me are impelled by their mental afflictions, how can anger toward them be justified? Their harmful acts arise from conditions, just as my own have done before.
Having thus developed diligence, I should place my mind in concentration. For the person whose mind is distracted dwells between the fangs of mental afflictions.
With the body and the mind kept in seclusion, distraction will not arise. Therefore, I should leave this worldly life and completely abandon all distracting thoughts.
All the suffering in the world arises from seeking pleasure for oneself. All the happiness in the world arises from seeking pleasure for others.
What need is there to say more? The childish work for their own benefit; the buddhas work for the benefit of others. Just look at the difference between them!
If I do not actually exchange my happiness for the suffering of others, I shall not attain the state of buddhahood, and even in samsara I shall have no joy.
Whatever joy there is in this world all comes from desiring others to be happy, and whatever suffering there is in this world all comes from desiring oneself to be happy.
Thus, the Buddha said that all the suffering of this world is born from self-cherishing, and all the happiness of this world is born from cherishing others.
By whatever merit I have gained through all of this, may every suffering of every living being be extinguished.
For all the sick, may I be their medicine. May I be their doctor and attend to them until their illness never recurs.
May I quell the pain of hunger and thirst with showers of food and drink. May I become both drink and food in the intermediate eons of famine.
For sentient beings, poor and destitute, may I become a treasure ever plentiful, and lie before them closely in their reach, a varied source of all that they might need.
For as long as space endures, and for as long as living beings remain, until then may I too abide to dispel the misery of the world.
Whatever suffering there is for living beings, may it all ripen upon me. And may the community of bodhisattvas always bring about the happiness of beings.
For all those ailing in the world, until their every sickness has been healed, may I myself become for them the doctor, the nurse, and the medicine itself.

title: Anapanasati Sutta: Mindfulness of Breathing
tagline: The Buddha's most detailed guide to meditation through mindful breathing.

verses.text: Thus have I heard. On one occasion the Blessed One was living at Savatthi in the Eastern Park, in the Palace of Migara's Mother, together with many well-known elder disciples.

Now on that occasion the Blessed One was seated in the open air, in the moonlight, on the Observance day, with the Sangha of monks sitting around him. Then, surveying the silent Sangha of monks, he addressed them thus:
"Monks, this assembly is free from idle chatter; this assembly is free from mere talk. It consists of pure heartwood. Such is this Sangha of monks, such is this assembly."

"Monks, mindfulness of breathing, when developed and cultivated, is of great fruit and great benefit. Mindfulness of breathing, when developed and cultivated, fulfills the four foundations of mindfulness. The four foundations of mindfulness, when developed and cultivated, fulfill the seven factors of enlightenment. The seven factors of enlightenment, when developed and cultivated, fulfill true knowledge and liberation."

sections.heading: The First Tetrad: Contemplation of the Body

verses.text: "And how, monks, is mindfulness of breathing developed and cultivated, so that it is of great fruit and great benefit? Here a monk, gone to the forest, or to the root of a tree, or to an empty hut, sits down. Having folded his legs crosswise, set his body erect, and established mindfulness in front of him, ever mindful he breathes in, mindful he breathes out."

"Breathing in long, he knows: 'I breathe in long.' Breathing out long, he knows: 'I breathe out long.' Breathing in short, he knows: 'I breathe in short.' Breathing out short, he knows: 'I breathe out short.'"
"He trains thus: 'I shall breathe in experiencing the whole body of breath.' He trains thus: 'I shall breathe out experiencing the whole body of breath.'"
"He trains thus: 'I shall breathe in tranquilizing the bodily formation.' He trains thus: 'I shall breathe out tranquilizing the bodily formation.'"

sections.heading: The Second Tetrad: Contemplation of Feelings

verses.text: "He trains thus: 'I shall breathe in experiencing rapture.' He trains thus: 'I shall breathe out experiencing rapture.'"
"He trains thus: 'I shall breathe in experiencing pleasure.' He trains thus: 'I shall breathe out experiencing pleasure.'"
"He trains thus: 'I shall breathe in experiencing the mental formation.' He trains thus: 'I shall breathe out experiencing the mental formation.'"
"He trains thus: 'I shall breathe in tranquilizing the mental formation.' He trains thus: 'I shall breathe out tranquilizing the mental formation.'"

sections.heading: The Third Tetrad: Contemplation of the Mind

verses.text: "He trains thus: 'I shall breathe in experiencing the mind.' He trains thus: 'I shall breathe out experiencing the mind.'"
"He trains thus: 'I shall breathe in gladdening the mind.' He trains thus: 'I shall breathe out gladdening the mind.'"
"He trains thus: 'I shall breathe in concentrating the mind.' He trains thus: 'I shall breathe out concentrating the mind.'"
"He trains thus: 'I shall breathe in liberating the mind.' He trains thus: 'I shall breathe out liberating the mind.'"

sections.heading: The Fourth Tetrad: Contemplation of Dharmas

verses.text: "He trains thus: 'I shall breathe in contemplating impermanence.' He trains thus: 'I shall breathe out contemplating impermanence.'"
"He trains thus: 'I shall breathe in contemplating fading away.' He trains thus: 'I shall breathe out contemplating fading away.'"
"He trains thus: 'I shall breathe in contemplating cessation.' He trains thus: 'I shall breathe out contemplating cessation.'"
"He trains thus: 'I shall breathe in contemplating relinquishment.' He trains thus: 'I shall breathe out contemplating relinquishment.'"
"Monks, that is how mindfulness of breathing is developed and cultivated, so that it is of great fruit and great benefit."

title: Lotus Sutra: The Parable of the Burning House
tagline: One of the most influential Mahayana sutras — all beings can attain buddhahood.

verses.text: At that time the World-Honored One calmly arose from his samadhi and addressed Shariputra: "The wisdom of the buddhas is infinitely profound and immeasurable. The door to this wisdom is difficult to understand and difficult to enter. Not one of the voice-hearers or pratyekabuddhas is able to comprehend it."

"Why is this? A buddha has personally attended hundreds of thousands of billions of buddhas, exhaustively carrying out all the immeasurable ways of the dharma, forging ahead with courage and vigor, his name universally known."
"He has realized the Law that is profound and never known before, and preaches it in accordance with what is appropriate, yet his intention is difficult to understand."

"Shariputra, ever since I attained buddhahood I have through various causes and conditions, various similes and parables, broadly expounded my teachings, and through countless skillful means have guided living beings, enabling them to let go of their attachments."
"Why is this? The Tathagata is fully possessed of both skillful means and the perfection of wisdom."

"Shariputra, the wisdom of the Tathagata is broad and great, profound and far-reaching. His mind is immeasurable. He has all the various paramitas, the power of fearlessness, meditation, liberation, and samadhi, and has deeply entered into the boundless and has awakened to the Law never before attained."

"Shariputra, the Tathagata knows how to make various distinctions and to expound the teachings skillfully. His words are soft and gentle and can delight the hearts of the assembly. Shariputra, to sum it up: the Buddha has fully realized the unlimited, boundless, and unprecedented dharma."

"But stop, Shariputra, I will say no more. Why? Because what the Buddha has achieved is the rarest and most difficult-to-understand dharma. The true aspect of all phenomena can only be understood and shared between buddhas. This reality consists of the appearance as such, the nature as such, the entity as such, the power as such, the activity as such, the causes as such, the conditions as such, the effects as such, the rewards and retributions as such, and the complete fundamental coherence of all these."

sections.heading: Chapter 3: The Parable of the Burning House

verses.text: At that time Shariputra, his mind dancing with joy, immediately stood up, pressed his palms together, and looking up at the World-Honored One, said: "Now, hearing the voice of the dharma from the World-Honored One, my heart is filled with joy, for I have gained what I never had before."

The Buddha said to Shariputra: "Suppose in a certain town in a certain country there was a very rich man. He was well along in years, and his wealth was immeasurable. He had many fields, houses, and servants."

"His own house was big and rambling, but it had only one gate. A great many people — a hundred, two hundred, perhaps as many as five hundred — lived in the house. The halls and rooms were old and decaying, the walls crumbling, the pillars rotten at their base."
"All at once and from every side, fire broke out and the house was engulfed in flames. The rich man's sons — ten, twenty, perhaps thirty of them — were inside the house."

"When the rich man saw the huge flames leaping up on every side, he was greatly alarmed and thought: 'Although I can escape to safety through the burning gate, my sons are inside the house absorbed in their games, unaware, unknowing, not alarmed, not frightened. The fire is closing in on them, suffering is about to overtake them, yet their minds have no sense of urgency and they have no thought of trying to escape!'"

"The rich man thought to himself: 'I have strength in my body and arms. I could wrap them in a covering and carry them out of the house.' But then again he thought: 'This house has only one gate and that is narrow and small. My sons are young, they have no understanding, and they love their games, being so engrossed in them that they are likely to be burned.'"

"Then the father thought: 'I must speak to them of the fearful danger. The house is already in flames. I must get them out quickly before they are burned.' So the father called to his sons: 'Come out at once, all of you!' But the father, though he pitied them and gave good words of warning, could not get them to come out, for the sons went on with their games and paid no heed."

"At that time the father thought: 'This house is already in flames. Unless my sons and I escape at once, we are certain to perish in the fire. I must think of some skillful means to get my sons out of here.' The father, knowing what his sons usually delighted in and all the various toys they were attracted to, said to them: 'The kind of playthings you like are hard to find. If you do not take them now, you will surely regret it later. Outside the gate there are goat-carts, deer-carts, and ox-carts for you to play with. Come out of this burning house at once and I will give you whatever you want!'"
"At that time the sons, hearing the father speak of these alluring playthings, eagerly pushed and shoved one another and came stampeding out of the burning house."

"At that time the rich man, seeing that his sons had gotten out safely and were all seated in the open square and were no longer in danger, was greatly relieved. Then each of the sons said to his father: 'The playthings you promised us, please give them to us now!' And the father gave to each of them a great ox-cart — the finest of all carriages, adorned with jewels, hung with bells, with garlands of flowers — for there was no reason to give them anything less."

"Shariputra, what do you think? Was that rich man guilty of falsehood in first having promised his sons three types of carts and then giving them only great carts? Shariputra said: 'No, World-Honored One. There was no falsehood, for he was merely using a skillful means to get his sons out of the burning house and thus save their lives.'"

"Shariputra, the Tathagata is likewise the father of all the world. He has forever left behind all fear, distress, grief, ignorance, and blindness. He has fully realized immeasurable perception, power, and fearlessness, he is endowed with great supernatural powers and the power of wisdom. He has fully mastered the means of skillfulness and the perfection of wisdom. His great compassion never rests. He constantly seeks what is good and what will benefit all."

"The three worlds are no safe place, just like the burning house, filled with many sufferings and truly to be feared, with the constant griefs and pains of birth, old age, sickness, and death, fires such as these raging endlessly. The Tathagata has already left the burning house of the three worlds, dwelling in peace and tranquility in the safety of forests and meadows."
"Now these three worlds are all my domain, and the living beings in them are all my sons. Now this place is filled with calamities, and I am the only person who can save and protect them."

title: The Tibetan Book of the Dead
tagline: A guide for navigating the intermediate states between death and rebirth.

sections.heading: The Moment of Death: The Luminosity of the Ground

verses.text: O nobly-born, listen with full attention, without being distracted. There are six states of existence: the god state, the demigod state, the human state, the animal state, the hungry ghost state, and the hell state. These are the six.

O nobly-born, the time has come for you to seek the path. Your breathing is about to cease. In the past your teacher has set you face to face with the Clear Light; now you are about to experience it in its Reality.

The first thing to be recognized is this: your own consciousness, shining, void, and inseparable from the great body of radiance, has no birth and no death. It is the immutable light, which the Tibetans call Buddha Amitabha, the Boundless Light.
Knowing this is sufficient. Recognizing the voidness of your own mind as buddhahood, and looking upon it as your own consciousness, is to keep yourself in the state of the divine mind of the Buddha.

When you are dying, the signs of death will come in a fixed order: the earth element sinks into water, and you experience heaviness; the water element sinks into fire, and your mouth and nose become dry; the fire element sinks into air, and your warmth fades; the air element sinks into consciousness, and your outbreath becomes longer than your inbreath.

sections.heading: The Bardo of Dharmata: Visions of Peaceful and Wrathful Deities

verses.text: O nobly-born, for three and a half days you will be in a faint. When you awaken, you will wonder what has happened. Recognize this as the bardo state. At that time, all of samsara will be in revolution. You will see lights and forms, and everything will be vivid and startling.

On the first day, the pure luminosity of the dharmadhatu, a brilliant blue light, will shine from the central realm. From the heart of Vairochana, a blue light will radiate toward you, blazing and clear, adorned with discs and smaller discs of light, dazzling and magnificent. Do not be afraid.

This is the light of the Tathagata called the Light of the Wisdom of the Dharmadhatu. Be drawn to it with faith and devotion. If you recognize it as the natural radiance of your own mind, without any need for prayer or devotion, you will merge with it and attain buddhahood.

On the second day, a white light of mirror-like wisdom will appear. On the third day, a yellow light of the wisdom of equality. On the fourth day, a red light of discriminating wisdom. On the fifth day, a green light of all-accomplishing wisdom. Do not be frightened by any of these.

O nobly-born, with each of these brilliant lights a dim, smoky light will also appear alongside. This dim light comes from the corresponding realm of samsara. Do not be attracted to the dim lights. They lead to further wandering. Be drawn instead to the brilliant, radiant lights — they are the path to liberation.
If you recognize these lights as the self-radiance of your own awareness, the natural display of your own mind, then without a moment's doubt you will be liberated.

sections.heading: The Bardo of Becoming: Seeking Rebirth

verses.text: O nobly-born, if you have not been liberated by the recognitions described above, then through the force of karma the bardo of becoming will dawn upon you. In this bardo you will have a body that is mental — luminous, unimpeded, endowed with miraculous powers. You can move anywhere in an instant, pass through mountains and walls.

Your mental body in this bardo state will be roughly the shape of your former life, with all your senses intact. You can see others but they cannot see you. You will feel the wind of karma blowing behind you, driving you forward.

O nobly-born, in this bardo you will see visions of men and women joined in union. When you see them, do not enter between them. If you are to be born as a male, you will feel attraction to the mother and aversion to the father. If you are to be born as a female, you will feel attraction to the father and aversion to the mother.

O nobly-born, when the visions of the bardo of becoming appear, remember these key instructions: do not be distracted. Hold to the thread of awareness. If distraction carries you, meditate on compassion. If you cannot meditate, simply rest in awareness without grasping.

Even at this stage, if you can recognize the clear light of the mind's true nature, liberation is possible. Rest in awareness. Let go of all attachment and aversion. In the nature of mind, there is nothing to be feared.

O nobly-born, concentrate your mind single-pointedly. Make a strong effort. If you meditate, you can still attain a good rebirth. Close the door to the womb by remembering your teacher and the Three Jewels. Cultivate compassion and bodhicitta. This is supremely important.
